title: Probability basics: events, AND, OR, independence
intro: Before computing anything fancy, let's master the basic language of chance. Just a few simple rules — how to combine probabilities of events: add them, multiply them, take the complement — underpin almost everything that follows, from A/B tests to spam filters. We'll work them out on a clear example: a roll of a die with six equally likely outcomes.

beats.text: Take a die roll: six equally likely outcomes. An event is a set of outcomes you care about: "even" = {2,4,6}, "greater than three" = {4,5,6}. Use the A and B buttons under the cells to define two events (by default A is the evens, B is above-three). The Venn diagram above lays out all six outcomes: the left circle is A, the right is B, the shared lens holds outcomes that fall into both. The probability P(A) is the share of the event's cells: 3 out of 6 = 0.5.

Rule one: the complement. P(not A) = 1 − P(A): everything that did NOT make it into A (the dash-outlined cells outside the blue circle). It often simplifies counting: "at least one" is computed through "none", P(at least one) = 1 − P(none) — enumerating directly is tedious, the complement makes it easy.

The OR rule (union). P(A∪B) = P(A) + P(B) − P(A∩B). The union is highlighted on the Venn now — every cell that fell into at least one event. The green lens (outcomes in both) gets subtracted — otherwise it would be counted twice. The formula under the die recomputes live as you change A and B. For mutually exclusive events (the lens is empty, the circles do not overlap) there is nothing to subtract: P(A∪B) = P(A) + P(B).
The AND rule (intersection) and independence. The intersection A∩B is the green lens on the Venn (outcomes in both events at once). Events are called independent when P(A∩B) equals EXACTLY P(A)·P(B).
beats.predict: Use the buttons to pick A and B so that "independent" lights up below. Is it easy?
beats.reveal: Not very — most pairs on a single die are dependent. An independent example: A = {1,2}, B = {1,3,5}: P(A∩B) = 1/6 = (1/3)·(1/2). For independent events P(A∩B) = P(A)·P(B) — probabilities multiply. That is how you get "two heads in a row" = 0.5·0.5 = 0.25 and five in a row = 0.5⁵ ≈ 3%. Naive Bayes and A/B math stand on this multiplication.

beats.text: What if the events are DEPENDENT? Then P(A∩B) = P(A)·P(B | A), where P(B | A) is the probability of B given that A has ALREADY happened. That is conditional probability — the bridge to the next lesson, where we will see how it deceives on rare events (medical tests, fraud detection).

definitions.text: an outcome is a single result of an experiment; an event is a set of outcomes you care about.

definitions.formula: P(A \cup B) = P(A) + P(B) - P(A \cap B)
definitions.text: for mutually exclusive events the intersection is 0.
one event does not change the probability of the other. If it does — the events are dependent.

practice: These rules are not school formalities — they are an analyst's daily arithmetic. "The chance that at least one of 1000 users hits a rare bug (p=0.1%)" is computed through the complement: 1 − 0.999¹⁰⁰⁰ ≈ 63%. Intuition fails here; the rule does not.

Multiplying independent probabilities also explains multiple comparisons (20 tests → the chance of at least one false alarm is 1 − 0.95²⁰ ≈ 64%) and why long coincidences are suspicious. Independence is the key — and often false — assumption, always worth a look.
realLife: System reliability: failure probabilities of duplicated independent nodes multiply, which is why redundancy slashes risk — fault-tolerance engineering stands on this.
Games and lotteries: jackpot odds are a product of independent probabilities, hence their astronomical rarity. The same rules power risk pricing in insurance.
